one_line_takeaway: TRACESAFE-BENCH shows that today's LLM-as-guard and specialized guardrail models fail to reliably intercept unsafe multi-step tool-calling traces mainly because they lack structured-data (JSON/code) parsing competence, not because they lack safety alignment -- guardrail performance tracks coding/structured-hallucination benchmarks far more than jailbreak-robustness benchmarks.
key_finding: Three headline results, all reported with the paper's own numbers: (1) Structural bottleneck -- TRACESAFE-BENCH performance correlates strongly with RAGTruth Data2txt (rho=0.80, reported as 0.7914 in Figure 3) and LiveCodeBench (rho=0.63, 0.6271 in Figure 3), but shows near-zero correlation with StrongREJECT jailbreak robustness (rho=0.05, 0.0509 in Figure 3) and only weak correlation with GPQA (rho=0.4499) and IFBench (rho=0.2632) -- indicating guardrail success on agentic traces is driven by structured-data/code parsing competence rather than moral/safety alignment. (2) Architecture over scale -- general-purpose LLMs-as-guards substantially outperform dedicated safety guardrails; e.g. in binary classification with schema, specialized guardrails Llama3-8B-Guard and Granite3.3-8B-Guard have benign-class rejection rates below 3% but unsafe-class detection below ~20-33% on several categories, while general LLM Qwen3-1.7B reaches 98.96% unsafe-class accuracy (though at the cost of 0% benign accuracy, i.e. rejecting everything); within the Qwen3 family (1.7B-32B) there is no monotonic scaling trend, and GPT-5 mini achieves 86.36% unsafe / 17.05% benign accuracy under binary w/o-schema. (3) Temporal stability/growth -- detection accuracy stays roughly flat up to ~22k trace characters and then rises at the longest percentiles, and models consistently score higher on trajectories with >=15 tool-calling steps than on 5-step trajectories, which the authors attribute to a shift from static tool-schema dominance toward dynamic behavioral signal as traces lengthen. A secondary finding: fine-grained (13-class) classification exposes that models detect structurally obvious/explicit risks (categories 1-5: prompt injection, privacy leakage) far better than subtle operational anomalies (categories 6-12, e.g. Interface Inconsistencies), with Qwen3-14B scoring just 4.71% on category 12 (Function Description Mismatch) despite 83.20%/83.58% overall coarse-grained unsafe/average accuracy.
core_contribution: The paper introduces TRACESAFE-BENCH, the first static, trace-level benchmark for evaluating LLM guardrails on multi-step agentic tool-calling trajectories (rather than single-turn prompts/responses or dynamic end-to-end agent simulators). Its own contribution is (1) a novel 'Benign-to-Harmful Editing' construction methodology -- a deterministic Check-and-Mutate pipeline that takes verified-correct multi-step trajectories from the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard (BFCL) and injects localized, step-level mutations to create harmful variants with precise ground-truth labels, avoiding both artificial free-form harmful generation and prohibitively expensive human annotation; (2) a 12-category risk taxonomy across four domains (prompt injection, privacy leakage, hallucination/environment-grounding, interface inconsistencies); and (3) a large-scale empirical evaluation of 13 general-purpose LLMs-as-guards and 7 specialized guardrail models (over 1,000 unique execution instances, 90 sampled traces per risk category) across four evaluation settings (binary w/o schema, binary w/ schema, coarse-grained multi-class, fine-grained multi-class).
threat_model: The paper targets guardrail/monitor models sitting between an agentic LLM and its tool-execution environment (MCP-style host/client/server architecture), whose job is to intercept unsafe intermediate tool-calling steps before they reach the environment -- distinct from evaluating the underlying agent's own robustness. Adversary-controlled surfaces modeled include: (a) a malicious/compromised tool provider that poisons a tool's description with an injected instruction (Prompt Injection-In); (b) a malicious external data source (e.g., a webpage) returning an adversarial payload in a tool's execution output (Prompt Injection-Out); (c) exfiltration paths where a benign-seeming tool call ends up leaking user PII or a fabricated API key/credential that has no functional need in that tool call (Privacy Leakage subtypes); and (d) non-adversarial but safety-relevant operational failure modes -- hallucinated tool names/arguments, missing type hints, redundant arguments, deprecated/version-conflicting tools, and description-name mismatches -- that the paper explicitly frames as part of the guardrail's job even though they are not attacker-driven.
limitations: The paper's own stated scope limits: benign seeds are drawn solely from BFCL, so trajectory diversity and tool-call realism are bounded by that leaderboard's task distribution; mutations are synthetically injected via a programmatic Check-and-Mutate pipeline rather than arising from real-world attacker behavior, so ecological validity rests on the claim that structural edits mimic 'realistic failure modes' rather than on observed incidents; only 90 traces are sampled per risk category (1,000+ instances total) for final evaluation, a modest scale per category for fine-grained (13-way) classification; LLM assistance was used for payload generation and data/code implementation, with the authors stating results were manually audited but not describing a formal audit protocol; not all models could be evaluated on every setting due to fixed risk taxonomies or output-format constraints in certain guardrails (noted in Table 1's caption), which limits full cross-model comparability in some settings; and the benchmark evaluates the guardrail/monitor in isolation on statically truncated traces, explicitly not evaluating the downstream agent's own robustness or dynamic multi-turn interaction with a live environment.

datasets_used: TRACESAFE-BENCH (paper's own contribution): 12 risk categories, 4 domains, 1,000+ unique execution instances, 90 sampled mutated traces per risk category plus benign traces
Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard (BFCL) multi-step split -- source of benign seed trajectories
GPQA -- external correlation benchmark (general question answering)
StrongREJECT -- external correlation benchmark (jailbreak robustness, converted to 1-ASR)
RAGTruth (Data2txt split) -- external correlation benchmark (structured hallucination detection)
quantitative_results: Correlation of TraceSafe F1 with RAGTruth Data2txt: rho=0.7914 (reported in text as 0.80)
Correlation of TraceSafe F1 with LiveCodeBench: rho=0.6271 (reported in text as 0.63)
Correlation of TraceSafe F1 with GPQA: rho=0.4499
Correlation of TraceSafe F1 with IFBench: rho=0.2632
Correlation of TraceSafe F1 with StrongREJECT robustness: rho=0.0509 (reported in text as 0.05)
Qwen3-1.7B (binary w/o schema): 98.96% unsafe-class accuracy, 0.00% benign-class accuracy, 49.48% balanced avg
Qwen3-14B (coarse-grained multi-class): 83.20% unsafe, 83.95% benign, 83.58% overall balanced accuracy (field-leading in that setting)
Qwen3-14B (fine-grained multi-class, category 12 Function Description Mismatch): 4.71% accuracy
GPT-5 mini (binary w/o schema): 86.36% unsafe accuracy, 17.05% benign accuracy, 51.70% balanced avg
GCP guardrail (binary w/o schema): 85.73% unsafe accuracy, 20.00% benign accuracy, 52.87% balanced avg
Llama3-8B-Guard (binary w/o schema): 19.21% unsafe accuracy, 97.53% benign accuracy, 58.37% balanced avg

Detection accuracy roughly flat up to ~22,000-character traces, then rises at longest percentiles; models consistently outperform baseline on trajectories with >=15 steps versus 5-step trajectories (Figure 4)
baselines_compared: 13 general-purpose LLMs-as-guards (open- and closed-weight), including gpt-oss-120b, GPT-5 mini, GPT-5.4 mini, Gemini3-Flash, Gemini3.1-Flash, Qwen3 family (0.6B/1.7B/4B/8B/14B/32B), Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3B, Ministral-14B, ToolACE-8B
7 specialized guardrail models (open- and closed-source), including Llama3-8B-Guard, Granite3.3-8B-Guard, Qwen3-0.6B/4B/8B guardrail variants, AWS Bedrock guardrail, GCP guardrail
